```markdown
| Project Proposal Details                                                                                     |
|--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Duration: The project will be for a period of 12 months from the date of signing of MOU.                   |
| The procurement and deployment of equipment proposed to be completed in one month from the date of signing of MOU. |
| Project Outcomes: This initiative will help the hearing-challenged children (i.e. beneficiary) to make their lives meaningful and look towards LIFE with equal opportunity, making them self-reliant, become part of mainstream society and valuable assets to their family, community, and the nation. The Beneficiaries stand a better chance of education, employment, and become valuable members of their families. |
| Target Beneficiaries: Hearing Challenged children of Premala Bai Chaavn Deaf and Dumb School, Karkardooma, New Delhi. SCF would obtain authentic data of registered disabled beneficiaries from the official website of the Ministry of Social Justice and Empowerment and local-level assessment. Tracking shall be done through UDID & Aadhar of the respective beneficiary. The survey report as submitted by SCH mentions the names of 150 students. |
| Monitoring & Reporting Mechanism: SCF shall be asked to provide the following:                             |
| A. The list showing receipt of "hearing aids" by the respective student along with their signature.         |
| B. The photograph of the event distributing the hearing aids.                                               |
| C. To share the quarterly progress report regarding benefits to the student on account of the subject project. |
| Further, the monitoring & reporting of the project will be done by PFT based on documents provided by SCF and also by field visits including interaction with the beneficiary. |
| Project Visibility:                                                                                          |
| a) SCF will ensure PFT/PTC brand name is visible at all locations covered under the subject project.       |
| b) SCF will put a banner with logo during the Assessment, Hearing Aid Fitting Camp, and Treatment etc.    |
| c) SCF will put the PFT/PTC brand in all asset purchases under the project for long term.                  |
| SCF shall be requested to provide the photos of above actions taken by them towards visibility.             |
| Project cost proposed to be implemented through "Swaroop Charitable Foundation":                            |
| The total cost of the project for the period of 12 months duration would not exceed Rs                      |
```

### Project Activities

| Project Activities                                                                                     |
|-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Identification of plantation sites                                                                   |
| Cleaning, soil testing, and layout designing of the site                                              |
| Pits digging as per layout                                                                            |
| Plantation of saplings at the identified locations                                                    |
| Geo-tagging of plantation site                                                                        |
| Give Me Trees Trust will be responsible for maintenance, Labour, watering, manuring and replacement of sapling in case it is damaged due to any reason (like storm, damage by animal etc.) |
| The complete process will be 100% organic in nature, using dense tree planting style methods with 0% pesticide usage to grow a dense forest. |
| The Give Me Trees Trust team will take full care of the planted saplings for the stipulated project duration of 3 years. After 3 years of planting, the trees become self-sufficient. |
| Give Me Trees Trust will also deploy a field team to keep a constant check on the planted saplings to make sure that they remain healthy. |
| Give Me Trees will provide quarterly/annual reports to PTC India, informing about project progress and the health of the saplings. |
| GMT will also conduct trainings on nature education for employees (online and offline, whatever is your preference), like tree planting, waste management, seed collection and various other environmental trainings. |
| PTC India can also conduct plantation drives as and when required.                                     |
| Give Me Trees will also make free-of-cost documentaries and videos on the project, showcasing how the area has changed before and after planting. |

| A comprehensive monitoring mechanism will be implemented to ensure timely and quality delivery of wheelchairs to Delhi Metro. Key performance indicators will track delivery timelines. Regular project meetings with Delhi Metro will be held bi-weekly, monthly progress reports shall be submitted, and user feedback will assess satisfaction on a sampling basis. Stakeholders, including Delhi Metro representatives, suppliers, project managers, and quality control teams, will collaborate to ensure successful project execution, addressing any issues promptly through a defined escalation procedure. |
```

chunks.text:
## Anticipated Challenges and Mitigation Strategy

| Risk & Challenges                                                                 | Mitigation Strategy                                                                                                           |
|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| The intrusion of Nilgai and other grazing animals poses a significant threat to the success of the upcoming project. | To address this challenge, Give Me Trees Trust (GMTT) plans to implement robust chain-link fencing around the plantation site. This fencing will serve as a physical barrier to effectively prevent Nilgai and other grazing animals from intruding and causing damage to the young saplings and overall vegetation. |
| Another significant challenge for the upcoming plantation project arises from the exceedingly rocky terrain of the land. | To tackle the challenges posed by the rocky terrain, we will provide manual laborers with specialized tools designed for effective use in such conditions. Additionally, we will promote teamwork among the laborers to ensure the workload is distributed evenly. |
| A third challenge for the upcoming plantation project is the risk of damage to the saplings from adverse weather conditions, such as storms and summer heat, as well as potential harm caused by animals. | To enhance the success of the plantation efforts, the GMTT team will proactively replace any affected saplings with healthy alternatives. This approach will ensure the resilience and sustainability of the plantation. |
```

| S.NO | QUERY SOUGHT BY PTC | OPINION OF IICA FOUNDATION TRUST |
|------|---------------------|----------------------------------|
| A)   | If the project is not completed by March 2025, how the unspent amount for the respective EY is to be treated in accordance with the applicable laws, | In the present context, the FAQ (dated 25th August 2021) may be referred at Sl. No. 6.6 and 6.7, Gazette notification of 22nd January 2021 & amended Schedule VII of the companies act should be referred as and when required. |
|      | FAQ 6.6 - Does the Board have the power to abandon or modify an ongoing project within the permissible period of three years? | Answer: As per the provisions of the CSR Rules, the Board may abandon or modify an ongoing project, partially or wholly, under exceptional circumstances, during the prescribed project period as per the recommendation of its CSR Committee, and by providing reasonable justification to that effect. It is important to keep in mind that the maximum permissible period for an ongoing project is three years excluding the year of commencement. |
|      | FAQ 6.7 - Can fund earmarked for one project be used for another project? | Answer: Yes, the budget outlay dedicated to one project can be used against another project, provided, the Board of the company, on recommendation of the CSR Committee of the Board should modify/abandon the existing project under exceptional circumstances by a board resolution, giving reasonable explanation and record the alteration in the target spending and modify the same in accordance with actual remainder of the budget of the existing project in the unspent CSR account. |
|      | FAQ 6.3 - What is the maximum permissible time period for any ongoing project? Can the time period of an ongoing project be extended beyond the permissible period? | Answer: As per the definition of an ongoing project the maximum permissible time period shall be three financial years excluding the financial year in which it is commenced i.e (0+3) financial years. |
```

| 24.3  | IICA opinion to deal with delay in completion of PTC Vishram Sadan beyond March 2025 | - NBCC has issued Letter of Award on M/s Vee Kay Construction, Ghaziabad (UP) on 28.11.2023.
- Land has been taken over from LHMC&H and handed over to NBCC simultaneously on 4ᵗʰ March, 24.
- NDMC has issued the approval related to PTC Vishram Sadan, vide their letter dated 22ⁿᵈ Oct, 2024.
- Commission on Air Quality Management (CAQM) has ordered implementation of GRAP (s)-State -2 guidelines dated 21ˢᵗ Oct 2024 and frequently enforcing GRAP from time to time which is continually hampering the start of work in full swing.
- The project activities have started, and Old Structure has been demolished and debris is being removed from site. Further the excavation in part has also started.
- In line with minutes of 24ᵗʰ CSR Committee Meeting, to deal with delay in relation to construction of Vishram Sadan, the matter was taken up with NBCC, and this matter is discussed in detail at Agenda Item No.25.3 (i). |
| 24.6  | Re-designed project proposal on Strengthening Patient Care Services at Ramakrishna Mission Free Tuberculosis Clinic and Medical Centre- Karol Bagh, Delhi | - The Committee deliberated on the proposal and discussed that only the equipment which are directly related to patient care may be considered and revised proposal may be put before CSR Committee.
- Accordingly, the revised proposal is put at 25.3(ii) for the kind consideration of CSR Committee.
- As this project is against the already approved project, no additional fund allocation is required. |

Ekal Vidhyalaya (Literarily one teacher), is a movement that aims to brings basic education to rural and tribal children in remote villages. Local young man or Women (often from same village) is trained to teach children. It is a non-governmental not profit initiative. Place for running of project: BLSP runs Ekal Vidyalayas by some arrangement with the local body at respective villages. Presently, they are running 1,00,000 Nos of Ekal schools (Bifurcating Numbers of School in district wise, Name of School not submitted) in states i.e. Uttar Pradesh, Uttarakhand, Himachal Pradesh, Jammu, Punjab & Bihar. In brief Ekal vidalaya teach children to read, write, count, stay healthy respect culture and grow in society | | | | |

| 2    | Name of project | Strengthening Primary Healthcare and Maternal-Child Health Services in Remote Villages of Bageshwar District, Uttarakhand |
| 3    | Details of the Project | Introduction- As per proposal, the infrastructure of primary healthcare and maternity health service in the remote village of Bageshwar, Uttarakhand, is lacking due to the non-availability of doctors/ medical staff and medical facilities. This healthcare project will mainly cover three activities as follows: |
|      |             | 1. Conducting Health camps for the underprivileged community - |
|      |             |    a. Health Camps: A total 60 Medical camps in a year will be conducted in consultation with CMO/ Govt Hospital/ PHC, by deploying the Mobile health check-up van 22 seater beneficiating 12000+ people. |
|      |             |    b. Deployment of Medical Staff: Qualified doctors, nurses, pharmacist and lab technicians. |
|      |             |    c. Activities: Diagnostics on site, Distribution of free medicine. |
|      |             |    d. Referral to PHC/CHC: Patient will be referred to PHC/CHC or district hospital on case to case basis. One CHW per cluster will be approached and assigned to support and follow up. |
|      |             | 2. Strengthening primary health care and maternal- child health: |
|      |             |    a. Engagement of Child Health Worker (CHW)- 25 CHWs(Child Health Worker) will be recruited & trained in partnership with Govt. District Hospital in the field of maternal child health care. Each child health worker (CHW) will be assigned a cluster of villages. |
|      |             |    b. Distribution of CHW health Kit- Child health kit will be given a kit which includes Diagnostic tools, BP monitor, Glucometers, Thermometers, Stethoscopes etc. |
|      |             | 3. Distribution of Maternal nutrition supplement kit and Child nutrition kit- it includes Vitamin syrup, Iron, Vitamin D, Zinc supplements, and ready-to-use food packet, Antibiotic, ORS, BP. (50 Mother and 50 Children for 8 months in a year) |
|      |             | 4. ABHA Card (Ayushman Bharat Health Account)- Project is integrated digitally with ABHA (Ayushman Bharat Health Account) through a digital ID. All treatment will be linked with their ABHA Health Card. Under this project, 20,000 + ABHA card will be generated. |
|      |             | Total cost project for 2 years is Rs. 2.75 crore which is inclusive of 22 Seater Mobile medical VAN (along with medical equipment), costing to Rs. 71.50 lakhs. |
```

### Project Phases

| Step | Description |
|------|-------------|
| Step-1 | Project Initiation |
| i) | Obtain necessary approvals from the District Inspector of Schools (DIOS) and the Education Department for school selection. |
| ii) | Identify two schools that would benefit most from the smart classroom initiative. |
| iii) | Conduct a comprehensive needs assessment of each selected school. |
| iv) | Establish project office, recruit and orient project staff, and create communication channels with stakeholders. |
| v) | Collaborate with education authorities to ensure alignment with district education goals. |

| Step | Description |
|------|-------------|
| Step-2 | Infrastructure Development |
| i) | Wall Renovation and Enhancement. |
| ii) | Complete wall restoration and painting. |
| iii) | Installation of proper insulation. |
| iv) | Eco-Friendly Furniture Installation. |
| v) | Double-seater desks crafted from recycled plastic waste. |
| vi) | Teacher workstations incorporating sustainable materials. |

| Step | Description |
|------|-------------|

| i) | Interactive touch panels with 4K resolution. |

### Intended Outcomes

| Category            | Outcomes                                                                                     |
|---------------------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Increased Awareness  | Increased knowledge among community, parents, and healthcare workers about pediatric heart disease symptoms, risk factors, and the importance of early detection and treatment. Improved understanding of preventive measures which includes fetal heart screening. |
| Early Detection      | Increased participation in cardiac screening sessions among children in targeted communities. Early identification of heart conditions through screening tests such as electrocardiograms (ECG), echocardiograms, and physical examinations. |
| Timely Treatment     | Prompt referral of children with diagnosed heart conditions to specialized healthcare facilities for further evaluation and treatment. Reduced delays in accessing medical care and surgical interventions for children requiring treatment for heart disease. |
| Improved Health Outcomes | Successful treatment and management of heart disease in children, leading to improved cardiac function, symptom relief, and enhanced quality of life. Reduction in the incidence of complications associated with untreated or inadequately managed pediatric heart disease. |

## Page 156

```markdown
| Step | Activity Description                                                                                          |
|------|---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| 1    | Awareness Sessions (12 Sessions)                                                                              |
|      | Approximately 60-70 healthcare workers/session                                                                |
|      | Resource required for awareness and medical camps: Project team includes project coordinator, medical officer, and field coordinator. They will connect with dispensary centers and plan awareness sessions (1 per month). |
|      | Other sub-activities include:                                                                                 |
|      | - Develop educational materials: Create pamphlets, brochures, and posters explaining common types of pediatric heart diseases, symptoms, risk factors, and the importance of early detection and treatment. |
|      | - Organize community workshops: Conduct interactive sessions in government dispensaries, community centers, and healthcare facilities to educate healthcare workers (ASHA, Anganwadi) about early symptoms in pediatric heart disease. |
|      | - Utilize local NGOs: Engage NGOs that work locally with child health and education, government schools to spread the message and plan health camps. |
|      | - Post awareness sessions, medical camps will be planned in the same locality.                               |
| 2    | Health Camps (10 Camps): With not more than 100/camp                                                         |
|      | - Conduct screening sessions: Offer free initial cardiac screening for children in communities, including physical examinations, to identify potential underlying heart conditions. |
|      | - Provide counseling and education: Offer one-on-one consultations with medical staff to discuss screening results, educate families about the significance of early detection, and provide guidance on healthy lifestyle practices. |
|      | - Distribute educational materials: Ensure that participants receive informational resources about pediatric heart health, including how to recognize symptoms, seek appropriate medical care, and adopt heart-healthy habits. |
|      | - Identified children with any underlying heart disease are then referred to OPD for confirmatory echo diagnosis. |
| 3    | Treatment of Children with Heart Disease (15 Children)                                                        |
|      | - Confirmed cases are registered under CHF HRiDAAN treatment program on submission of mandatory due diligence documents which include: Ration card of family, Aadhaar card of parents, birth certificate of patient, address and income proof of parent, CHF Poverty index form. |
|      | - Facilitate treatment support: Identify the partner hospital for the particular patient, facilitate the treatment, and handhold the family throughout the hospital admission, treatment, and discharge. |
|      | - Post treatment stay and follow-up: Post treatment and discharge, patients are advised to stay close to the hospital until the first follow-up. |
```

### Step 1: Awareness Sessions

| **Activity**                                                                                     | **Details**                                                                                     |
|--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| i) Healthcare workers/session                                                                     | a) Resource required for awareness and medical camps: The project team includes the project coordinator, medical officer, and field coordinator. They will be involved in connecting with the dispensary centers and planning for awareness sessions (1 in one month). |
|                                                                                                  | b) Develop educational materials: Create pamphlets, brochures, and posters explaining common types of pediatric heart diseases, symptoms, risk factors, and the importance of early detection and treatment. |
|                                                                                                  | c) Organize community workshops: Conduct interactive sessions in government dispensaries, community centers, and healthcare facilities to educate healthcare workers (ASHA, Anganwadi) about early symptoms in pediatric heart disease. |
|                                                                                                  | d) Utilize local NGOs: Engage NGOs that work locally with child health and education, and government schools to spread the message and plan health camps. |
|                                                                                                  | e) Post-awareness sessions, the medical camps will be planned in the same locality.            |

### Activities to be done under the project step-wise

| **Step** | **Activity**                                                                                     |
|----------|--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| 1        | Conduct Awareness sessions regarding symptoms, early screening, and diagnosis for frontline workers and the community. |

| 3        | Treatment of Neonates, infants, and children in the age group of (0 to 18 years) for the diagnosed heart disease. |
| 4        | Handhold the families through the treatment process and provide follow-up till 18 years of age. |

## Page 153

```markdown
| ORGANISATION GOALS                                                                                                           |
|------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| 1. To treat each and every child with heart disease                                                                          |
| 2. Becoming a helping hand for the Government bodies for the treatment of marginalized children born with CHD                |
| 3. Create social awareness and generate philanthropy for these children.                                                    |
| 4. To uplift Pediatric Cardiology through technological and scientific advancement to achieve international standards of care |
| 5. Sharing the knowledge of Pediatric Cardiology amongst Medical Community to improve awareness and early diagnosis           |
| 6. To educate the next generation of leaders in child health and define and cascade steps for pediatric cardiac centres in all the cities of India which works specifically for under privileged people |

| ORGANISATIONAL OBJECTIVES                                                                                                    |
|------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| 1. Diagnosis of child heart disease (CHD), appropriate treatment of CHD with Internationally acceptable mortality and morbidity |
| 2. Pediatric cardiac treatment should be within reach of every child irrespective of their financial status or their educational status of parents, gender, caste, religion, or social and economic background |
| 3. To provide financial assistance to children from underprivileged families who cannot afford treatment/ surgeries. Thus, they can't approach private hospitals since the cost is exorbitant and in govt. hospitals, they have to wait for months, years for their turns and treatment which could be fatal for a child's health. |
| 4. To have regional Pediatric Cardiac Centres in all states where economically challenged patients can be treated.            |
```

chunks.text:
### Background Information

1.0 The subject project was presented by the Child Heart Foundation before the 25th CSR committee, wherein the CSR committee considered the subject project for In-Principle approval and desired to carry out the due diligence of the same and put up the detailed agenda including financial commitment before the next CSR committee.

1.1 Accordingly, Child Heart Foundation (CHF) has submitted the project proposal and submitted their confirmation vide letter dated 31st January 2025 for the project value of 20,00,000/- (copy attached at Annexure- I, Page No. 150). This letter is a supplement to the earlier submitted proposal, (copy of earlier proposal attached Annexure-II, Page 151-171).

2.0 Ministry of Corporate Affairs has identified various areas of sustainable development to be undertaken by Corporates for their CSR Initiatives under Schedule VII of Companies Act 2013. Schedule VII (i), includes CSR Initiatives such as “Promoting health care including preventive health”.

3.0 The Summary of relevant statutory documents pertaining to Child Heart Foundation is attached at Annexure- III, Page no.172.

3.1 For the purpose of reference towards experience with Govt. entity, Child Heart Foundation has worked with GAIL India Limited, providing Pacemakers to children with congenital heart disease belonging to the underprivileged society in Delhi for a value of Rs. 34 Lakhs under CSR.

| **Quantitative Indicators**  |                                                                                             |

| Health Outcomes              | Number of children who show improvement after treatment.                                   |
| **Qualitative Indicators**    |                                                                                             |

| Behavioural Change          | Changes in behaviour or practices, such as awareness about heart disease in children.      |

| Frequency                    | At least monthly / quarterly visits by the Donor’s monitoring team to the project sites.   |
| On-the-ground Monitoring      | Meetings with project managers, field staff, medical professionals, and beneficiaries.     |
| Verification of Activities    | Verifying if the scheduled medical camps, sessions, and treatments are being conducted as per the plan. |
| **Remote Monitoring**         |                                                                                             |
| Regular video calls/virtual meetings | With the project team to discuss progress and challenges.                               |
| Online Platforms             | Use of a project management tool (like Google Drive, MS Teams, or a dedicated M&E platform) to monitor real-time data on project activities, including attendance, outcomes, and treatment results. |

8. Funeral services whenever required. |

## Page 200

```markdown
| **Project Name**                                      | **Location**                                   | **Objective**                                                                                                           |
|-------------------------------------------------------|------------------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Production of Sanitary Napkins for women from marginalized communities | Sector 128, Noida and nearby sectors (Sultanpur village, Shahpur, Govardhanpur, Bajidpur, Asgarpur, Rohilapur, Bakhtawarpur, Raipur Khadar, etc, UP) | 1. Improve Menstrual Health & Hygiene by making sanitary napkins affordable and accessible.
2. No profit will be earned; free distribution to underprivileged girls and women.
3. Empowering women and girls, enhancing dignity and self-esteem.
4. Increase access to education without the burden of inadequate menstrual management.
5. Create employment opportunities for women in production, marketing, and distribution networks. |
```

| **Step** | **Activity**                                                                                     |

| 1     | Basic Amenity Program        | The basic needs of food and clothing and shelter have been met | Record of food distribution, Record of clothes distribution including winter clothes |
| 2     | Active Aging and Empowerment Program | Improved satisfaction and happiness quotient through Active Aging Programmes | Enhanced communication with volunteers, Increased participation/attendance in all the social and cultural activities, Feedback and Success Stories |

| Target Beneficiaries (Indirect & Direct) under this project |
|-------------------------------------------------------------|
| The project supports abandoned, elderly, destitute women mostly widows. The average age group of these women is 80 years and above living in Vrindavan and Radhakund. Neglected or abandoned by their families, due to death of spouse or other family circumstances, these women have flocked to Vrindavan over the years to find help and spend the last years of their life in spiritual/religious activities in the holy city. Many of them suffer from age-related ailments, like Dementia, Parkinson, Paralysis, Blindness and other critical illnesses that require constant help with their daily personal chores like washing, moving around, eating and medication. A detailed due diligence is carried out to assess the genuineness of the case. An identity card with photograph is provided to the eligible Mothers. The Trust maintains a database of these Mothers with all relevant details. So far 76 cases have been identified. |

| Monitoring & Reporting Mechanism by the Implementing Agency |
|--------------------------------------------------------------|
| Reports in pre-agreed format can be provided to the Donors. Utilization report certified by a CA shall be provided to all Donors. |

### Project Outcomes

| Outcome Type          | Description                                                                                          |
|-----------------------|------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Clinical Outcomes      | a) Improved Diagnostic Accuracy: Increase in the correct identification of conditions, such as tumors, fractures, or internal bleeding.
b) Reduced Diagnostic Errors: Decrease in false positives or negatives in imaging results
c) Enhanced Disease Management: Better ability to stage diseases and monitor treatment progress, particularly in cancer care. |
| Patient Outcomes       | a) Shorter Wait Times for patients.
b) Patient Satisfaction: Higher ratings of patient experience related to comfort, clear communication, and overall service.
c) Accessibility: Improved access for underserved populations, ensuring equitable healthcare. |
| Technical Outcomes     | a) Image Quality Improvement: Enhanced clarity and resolution of CT images, allowing for better visualization of anatomical structures.
b) Reduced Radiation |
| Financial Outcomes     | a) Cost Savings: Reduction in overall imaging costs through efficient resource use, limiting unnecessary imaging, and reducing repeat scans. |

### Activities Table

| Step | Activity Description                                                                                     |
|------|---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| I    | Pre-production phase: Conducting a need assessment program by field survey and awareness program on menstrual hygiene. |
| II   | Planning: Identifying different manufacturing units/suppliers, visiting the unit, assessing cost of production, checking permissions, arranging funds, and placing orders. |
| III  | Production phase: Hiring space and staff, setting up a production unit, training staff, sourcing raw materials, starting production, assessing wastages, and adhering to compliance. |
| IV   | Distribution phase: Building a network of local sales agents, conducting awareness campaigns, and monitoring/evaluating the project's impact. |

### Project Proposals

| Equipment                          | Purpose                                                                                          |
|------------------------------------|--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Argos Optical Biometer Suits       | 41% greater penetration in harder grade cataracts. Helps to diagnose all types of eyes with accuracy. Highest acquisition rate in current available optical Bio meter. Precise Reading as compared to other Optical Biometers, to get accurate IOL power of Patience Eye. The only biometer to have a vision planner. Future-ready product with Di-Com connectivity. SS-OCT- Live OCT-based patient fixation and K1-K2 readings. |
| Luxor Microscope Suits             | 6 times WIDER red reflux zone. LONGER depth of field from the anterior surface of the Cornea till PC. Electromagnetic clutches. DMM (Digital Marker Microscope)-Combined part requirement to attach Microscope and Optical Biometer. Wireless connectivity with Centurion. Argos (Optical Biometer) will get integrated with the Centurion Phaco Machine. |
| Integrated 1 MOS Camera System      | Inbuilt medical grade camera with recording. HD camera with recording and live video playback screen. Inbuilt Video overlay in Microscope. |

### Payment Conditions

1. **Advance Payment**:

- This payment shall be made after the delivery and installation of equipment at the hospital and shall be as follows:
- Labhmuni shall submit the following for requesting PFT to make payment:

## Page 21

```markdown
| **Objective of the Project**                                                                                                           |
|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| The objective of the "Support for CT Scan Machine at Safdarjung Hospital, New Delhi" project is to enhance the capabilities of the Department of Radiodiagnosis and Interventional Radiology. The CT scan machine is a vital tool for diagnostic imaging, serving a large number of patients who rely on the hospital's services. Safdarjung Hospital, located in the heart of New Delhi on Ring Road, is the largest central government hospital in India, with a capacity of 3,000 beds. It is affiliated with Vardhman Mahavir Medical College and serves as a key healthcare provider, offering comprehensive care across more than 15 medical specialties. Currently, the hospital has only one CT scan machine, which is primarily used by critical departments such as Trauma, Emergency, and Stroke care. This limited capacity often leads to significant delays in diagnostic procedures, resulting in the loss of crucial time for patients in need of immediate care. With an average daily footfall of 15,000 to 18,000 patients, the addition of a second CT scan machine would greatly alleviate waiting times and enable the hospital to serve a larger number of patients more efficiently. Key objectives include: Detailed Imaging, Disease diagnosis, Guidance for treatment, Contrast imaging, etc. |

| **Location of the Project** | Safdarjung, Delhi                                                                 |
|-----------------------------|---------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| **Activities to be done under the project step-wise** |                                                                                 |
| A) Project Planning and Coordination |                                                                                 |
| Detailed Project Planning | Develop a comprehensive project plan, including timelines, milestones, and resource allocation. |
| Stakeholder Coordination | Coordinate with Safdarjung administration, to align project goals, activities, and requirements. |
| Compliance and Permits | Ensure all necessary permits, certifications, and regulatory approvals are obtained to initiate renovations and equipment installations. |
| B) Procurement and Installation of Equipment |                                                                                 |
| Procurement process | Follow DFY procurement policy. |
| Vendor Selection and Procurement | Select vendors for lab furniture, IT infrastructure, and other supplies through a tender process. |
| Quality Assurance | Ensure that all procured items meet the required standards for healthcare. |
| Equipment Installation and Testing | Oversee the setup and calibration of all new equipment ensuring that they are installed correctly and operational. |
| C) Monitoring, Evaluation, and Reporting |                                                                                 |
| Progress Monitoring | Develop and maintain a monitoring system to track project milestones, budget utilization, and overall progress. |
| Reporting to Stakeholders | Regularly update all stakeholders, including funders, hospital leadership, and relevant government bodies, on project progress, achievements, and challenges. |
```

## Page 52

```markdown
| Project Name                                                        | Empowering Marginalized and Vulnerable Youth through Skill Development Training |
|---------------------------------------------------------------------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Objective of the project                                             | To impart vocational skill development training to 400 marginalized and vulnerable youth from economically and socially marginalized families at migrant camps in Jagti, Purkhoo and Muthi (Jammu) and Srinagar (Kashmir). To promote confidence and teamwork among beneficiaries by incorporating aspects of soft skills and Personality Development in the course curriculum. To provide marginalized beneficiaries an opportunity to work as skilled professionals and become a part of economic growth. To provide sustainable livelihood opportunities to the trained beneficiaries in the form of placement or self-employment. |
| Location of the Project                                             | Migrant camps in Jagti, Purkhoo and Muthi (Jammu) and Srinagar (Kashmir)       |
| Activities to be done under the project step-wise                   |                                                                                  |
| A. Step-I - Needs Assessment and Mobilization                       | Conduct surveys to identify specific skills gaps and career opportunities at each sector and location. Engage with community stakeholders including local leaders, made aware of the training programme and mobilize the beneficiaries for the courses. |
| B. Step-2 - Training Setup and Curriculum Design                    | The six months training programs comprising of two segments i.e., classroom and practical. Establishing training infrastructure with necessary equipment. The course wise curriculum of National Skill Development Corporation (NSDC) will be followed for Apparel (tailoring) and IT/ITES (data entry) sectors. After successful completion of the course, beneficiaries will be awarded with NSDC certificates which are widely recognized by employers across India including leading companies and government organizations. |
| C. Step-3 - Training Delivery                                        | Training will be provided in Jagti, Purkhoo and Muthi (Jammu) and Srinagar (Kashmir) equipped with necessary infrastructure and equipment. Begin with four batches of 25 students in each course, targeting 400 participants per trade in 12 months. Deliver hands-on practical training sessions conducted by qualified instructors in all offered fields tailored to the specific needs and opportunities of such locations. |
```

### Anticipated Challenges/Risks and Mitigation Strategy

| Challenge                          | Risk Description                                                                 | Mitigation Strategy                                                                                     |
|------------------------------------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------|--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Low Participation and Retention Rates | Beneficiaries may hesitate to participate or drop out due to competing responsibilities. | Conduct community outreach, flexible training schedules, and motivational sessions.                     |
| Insufficient Job Placement Opportunities | Local job market may not have enough placement opportunities for all graduates. | Build partnerships with local businesses and offer entrepreneurial support, including micro-loans.     |
| Limited Access to Quality Trainers  | Difficulty in hiring qualified trainers may impact training quality.              | Invest in local trainer capacity-building, consider virtual training, and maintain a database of backup trainers. |

## Page 51

```markdown
| Step | Description                                                                                                           |
|------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| 4    | Soft Skills Training: Training on essential soft skills such as communication, interpersonal skills, teamwork, time management, customer service, and problem-solving skills. Provide coaching and mentorship opportunities to enhance participants' confidence, professionalism, and employability. |
| 5    | Job Placement Assistance and Entrepreneurship Support: Collaboration with local employers, job placement agencies, and entrepreneurship support organizations to facilitate job placements and self-employment opportunities for trained candidates. Provide guidance and support in resume writing, job interviews, and micro loans for self-employment. |
| 6    | Monitoring and Evaluation: Implementation of regular monitoring and evaluation mechanisms to assess the progress and effectiveness of the training programs. Collection of feedback from participants, trainers, and stakeholders to identify strengths, weaknesses, and areas for improvement, and make necessary adjustments to ensure program quality and impact. |
| 7    | Community Engagement and Outreach: Conduct community awareness campaigns to promote the project and encourage participation among marginalized and vulnerable youth. Foster partnerships with local community organizations to enhance project visibility and support sustainability efforts beyond the duration of the project. |
| 8    | Documentation and Reporting: Maintain comprehensive records of participant attendance, progress, and outcomes throughout the project duration. Prepare regular progress reports and documentation of project activities, achievements, and lessons learned for stakeholders, funders, and other relevant entities. |

| Duration of the Project | 12 Months |
|------------------------|-----------|

| Step | Project Outcomes                                                                                                   |
|------|--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| 1    | Enhanced Employability and Livelihood Opportunities: 400 marginalized youth from migrant camps at Jagti, Purkhoo, Muthi, and Srinagar to gain industry-relevant skills in tailoring (Apparel) and data entry (IT/ITES). Certified participants are better equipped for job placement or entrepreneurship, contributing to a sustainable income source. |
| 2    | Economic Upliftment and Poverty Reduction: Securing employment or starting their own businesses, increasing household income. |
```

chunks.text:
### Background Summary

1. The subject project was presented by the GreenWorld Education and Research (GWER) before the 25th CSR committee, wherein the CSR committee considered the subject project for In-Principle approval and desired to carry out the due diligence of the same and put up the detailed agenda including financial commitment before the next CSR committee.
2. Accordingly, GreenWorld Education and Research (GWER) has submitted the project proposal and their confirmation vide letter dated 29ᵗʰ January 2025 for the project value of 8,00,000/- (Copy attached at Annexure- I, Page No. 64). This letter is a supplement to the earlier submitted proposal, (Copy of earlier proposal attached Annexure-II, Page no. 65-80).
3. Ministry of Corporate Affairs has identified various areas of sustainable development to be undertaken by Corporates for their CSR Initiatives under Schedule VII of Companies Act 2013. Schedule VII (ii), includes CSR Initiatives such as “promoting education”.
4. The Summary of relevant statutory documents pertaining to GreenWorld Education and Research (GWER) is attached at Annexure- III, Page no 81.

### Project Proposal Details

| Step | Activity Description                                                                                     |
|------|---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| 1    | Community teacher training program and recruitment of appropriate teachers.                             |
| 2    | Adaptation of existing Life-Skills Curriculum and its implementation in the form of art integration in academic subjects and socio-emotional learning spaces. |
| 3    | Setting up Computer-Lab and conducting computer classes (a new addition proposed in the academic year 2025-26 on a pilot basis in one of the centers). |
| 4    | Keeping the infrastructure of the learning center in good shape through regular repair and maintenance works. |
| 5    | Continuous development, appraisal, and improvement of teaching learning materials and student workbooks and other learning materials. |
| 6    | Regular procurement of stationery, teaching-learning materials, textbooks, and reading books.          |
| 7    | Annual educational field trips for students; and other group activities (sports days).                 |
| 8    | Monitoring of the classroom teaching, and regular progress monitoring of learning outcomes of students and sharing with the parents and other stakeholders. |

| Procurement of eye equipment for Mahatma Gandhi Netralaya | The 'Mahatma Gandhi Netralaya' provides eye-care exclusively to the poor and underprivileged in district Alwar. Elderly individuals in rural areas remain untreated due to the absence of affordable facilities or the indifference of family members. Through outreach camps in remote rural areas, poor patients are identified and brought to the Netralaya for cataract surgery. Every year the hospital performs over 1000 surgeries. Till date, the hospital has performed over 5400 cataract surgeries. |

| Objective of the Project                                                                                                                                                                                                 |
|---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| To empower underprivileged older persons with vision loss by providing comprehensive eye care services, enabling their reintegration into mainstream society while addressing their holistic needs and contributing to the well-being of the entire community. |

| Location of the Project                |
|----------------------------------------|
| Village Kaduki, near Vijay Mandir, Alwar, Rajasthan. 301001 |

| Activities to be done under the project step-wise |
|----------------------------------------------------|
| 1. Organizing Outreach Camps                        |
|    - Conduct outreach camps twice a week in rural and underserved areas to identify patients with vision problems, particularly cataracts. |
|    - Deploy a team equipped with eye equipment to screen patients on-site. |
| 2. Patient Identification and Counseling           |
|    - Identify cataract patients during the camps.  |
|    - Provide counseling to patients and their families, emphasizing the importance of timely treatment and addressing any concerns. |
```

chunks.text:
### Project Proposals

| Project Title                                   | Description                                                                                          |
|------------------------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Transportation to Mahatma Gandhi Netralaya (MGN) | Arrange free transportation for identified patients from the camp location to MGN, ensuring they travel comfortably and safely. |
| Surgical Treatment at MGN                      | Perform cataract surgeries at MGN. Ensure high standards of hygiene, precision, and post-operative care during and after the surgery. |
| Post-Surgical Recovery and Care                 | Provide post-operative care, including monitoring in the recovery ward, medication, and guidance on eye care. Distribute vision correction glasses if required, ensuring optimal recovery and improved quality of life for patients. |
| Patient Return and Follow-Up                    | Transport patients back to the camp location the day after surgery, ensuring their comfort and well-being. Schedule follow-up visits during subsequent outreach camps to monitor progress and address any complications. |
| Equipment Procurement for Efficient Camp Management | To enhance the efficiency and effectiveness of eye camps, this proposal seeks support for the procurement of advanced ophthalmology equipment. The equipment will enable the Mahatma Gandhi Netralaya team to improve the precision and speed of on-site patient screening during camps and enhance diagnostic capabilities to ensure accurate identification of conditions requiring surgical intervention. This initiative aims to expand access to quality eye care for underserved communities, reducing the burden of avoidable blindness and restoring vision to those in need. |
| Reporting to Donor and Usage of Equipment      | **Reporting to Donor**: Quarterly Impact Reports: Provide donors with detailed reports, including the number of camps organized, patients screened, surgeries conducted, and the overall impact of the initiative. Annual Report: A comprehensive report including financial utilization, equipment usage data, and the program's impact will be submitted at the end of the financial year. **Usage of Equipment**: The newly procured ophthalmology equipment will be extensively used during outreach camps & in Netralaya for precise diagnosis and screenings. |

| Project Name                                     |
|-------------------------------------------------|
| Lighting the Future: Quality Education for Migrant Children |

| Objective of the Project                         |
|-------------------------------------------------|
| Delhi has roughly 18 lakh children 6 to 10 years old (Census 2011); almost half of these attend government-run primary schools. A majority of the students that attend government primary schools come from rural-to-urban migrant labourer families representing substantial linguistic and cultural diversity. The migration of their parents does not follow academic year timeline leading to low attendance rates. These students have substantial learning deficits that could not be easily addressed by regular government or private schools. Approximately 9% of India's overall population is comprised of rural-urban migrants mostly residing in big cities such as Delhi, Mumbai, Chennai, etc. Notwithstanding their difficult and uncertain work circumstances, many of these parents, often illiterate or poorly educated, aspire for better learning outcomes for their children and opt for private tuitions to fill the learning gaps left by poor quality of education as well as by their own. |
```

chunks.text:
### Risk and Mitigation Strategy

| Risk                                                        | Mitigation Strategy                                                                                     |
|-------------------------------------------------------------|--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Difficulty of recruiting/training sufficient skilled teachers within budget | Multiple channels of advertisement of vacancies will be used (job hai, Apna job, Naukri.com) besides through word of mouth. Non-monetary incentives and other benefits (EPF and ESIC) will be provided. |
| Security of tenure and condition of building to run the learning centre | Multi-year lease (at least for 5 years will signed.                                                  |
| Lack of interest of parents to attend weekend literacy classes | Appropriate communication strategies will be deployed.                                                |
| Deteriorating economic condition, leading to higher rates of back migration to villages or too frequent in-and out migration of the children | Developing strategies to stay in contact with student during their temporary migration to the villages and parent and student counselling to reduce the absenteeism and lower learning outcomes. |

## Page 98

```markdown
| Project Outcomes Step-Wise                          | Description                                                                                          |
|-----------------------------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| 1. Reduction in Preventable Blindness                | A significant decrease in cases of cataract-induced blindness among underprivileged individuals, particularly the elderly, in mural Alwar, Rajasthan. |
| 2. Improved Access to Quality Eye Care               | Enhanced access to affordable and advanced eye care services for underserved communities, ensuring timely diagnosis and treatment. |
| 3. Increased Outreach and Awareness                  | Greater community awareness about eye health through regular outreach camps, resulting in early identification and treatment of vision-related issues. |
| 4. Empowerment Through Restored Vision               | Improved quality of life for beneficiaries, enabling them to regain independence and contribute actively to their families and communities. |
| 5. Efficient Service Delivery                         | Enhanced operational efficiency through the use of advanced ophthalmology equipment, allowing the team to serve a higher number of patients effectively. |
| 6. Measurable Impact and Transparency                | Clear documentation of the program's impact through patient success stories, follow-up data, and comprehensive reporting to stakeholders and donors. |
```

### Project Outcomes Step-wise

| Step | Description                                                                                     |
|------|-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| 1    | All children in the underserved communities covered by the project get quality learning opportunities within their communities. |
| 2    | All elementary school age children (Nursery to V) will achieve age appropriate and grade level learning outcomes as measured by regular standardized tests. |
| 3    | Migrant children migrating in-between the sessions with substantial learning gaps will get appropriate remedial educational support to bring them to their level. |
| 4    | Unemployed women who have a passion to teach children will get a fair chance to get upskilled and work as a teacher. |

| Organizing Outreach Camps                     | a) Conduct outreach camps twice a week in rural and underserved areas to identify patients with vision problems, particularly cataracts.

| Patient Identification and Counselling        | a) Identify cataract patients during the camps.
b) Provide counseling to patients and their families, emphasizing the importance of timely treatment and addressing any concerns. |
| Transportation to Mahatma Gandhi Netralaya (MGN) | i. Perform cataract surgeries at MGN.                                                                                 |
| Surgical Treatment at MGN                    | a) Ensure high standards of hygiene, precision, and post-operative care during and after the surgery.                  |

| 3. Increased Outreach and Awareness                  | Greater community awareness about eye health through regular outreach camps, resulting in early identification and treatment of vision-related issues. |
| 4. Empowerment Through Restored Vision               | Improved quality of life for beneficiaries, enabling them to regain independence and contribute actively to their families and communities. |
| 5. Efficient Service Delivery                         | Enhanced operational efficiency through the use of advanced ophthalmology equipment, allowing the team to serve a higher number of patients effectively. |
| 6. Measurable Impact and Transparency                | Clear documentation of the program's impact through patient success stories, follow-up data, and comprehensive reporting to stakeholders and donors. |
```
